DevOps engineer at a SaaS startup. Obsessed with CI/CD, platform engineering, and Kubernetes. CNCF ambassador.
Senior backend engineer. Rust enthusiast. Previously at Mozilla on the Firefox performance team. Strong opinions about database design.
Full-stack developer. React on the frontend, Django on the back. Five years shipping products at early-stage startups.

Cloud architect. AWS and GCP certified. Building resilient infrastructure for fintech applications.
Security engineer and OWASP contributor. Specialises in API security, threat modelling, and secure SDLC practices.

Engineering manager, former SRE. Writes about on-call culture, incident retrospectives, and the human side of software reliability.
ML engineer. Python and PyTorch daily. Interested in making machine learning systems production-ready, not just notebook-ready.
Platform engineer. Terraform, Kubernetes, and GitOps. Building the developer platform so product teams can ship without worrying about infra.

fields.title: Getting Started with FastAPI: Build Your First REST API
fields.body:
## Why FastAPI?

FastAPI has become the default choice for new Python REST APIs, and for good reason. It is built on Starlette (async web) and Pydantic (data validation), giving you automatic request validation, serialisation, and interactive OpenAPI docs at `/docs` with zero configuration.

Benchmarks consistently put FastAPI 2–4x faster than Flask and Django REST Framework on I/O-bound workloads, on par with Node.js Express.

## Your First Endpoint

```python
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()

class Item(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: float
    in_stock: bool = True

@app.post('/items/', status_code=201)
async def create_item(item: Item):
    return {'name': item.name, 'status': 'created'}
```

Send `price: 'abc'` and you get a clear 422 Unprocessable Entity back—Pydantic handles validation before your function is even called.

## Dependency Injection

FastAPI's DI system is one of its most underrated features. Database sessions, auth tokens, and feature flags are all injected cleanly:

```python
from fastapi import Depends
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

def get_db():
    db = SessionLocal()
    try:
        yield db
    finally:
        db.close()

@app.get('/posts/{post_id}')
async def get_post(post_id: int, db: Session = Depends(get_db)):
    post = db.query(Post).filter(Post.id == post_id).first()
    if not post:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail='Not found')
    return post
```

Dependencies are composable—stack auth on top of the DB session, then stack rate limiting on top of auth.

## Background Tasks

For fire-and-forget work (sending emails, writing audit logs) use `BackgroundTasks`:

```python
from fastapi import BackgroundTasks

def send_welcome_email(email: str):
    # your email logic here
    pass

@app.post('/users/')
async def create_user(user: UserCreate, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    db_user = create_user_in_db(user)
    background_tasks.add_task(send_welcome_email, user.email)
    return db_user
```

## Verdict

FastAPI is an excellent default for new Python APIs in 2025. Type safety, automatic docs, and async performance are hard to beat. Start with a single router and grow from there.
fields.excerpt: FastAPI combines Python type hints, Pydantic validation, and async support to give you a modern, high-performance REST API framework with zero boilerplate.

fields.title: Docker Compose for Local Development: A Complete Guide
fields.body:
## The Problem

Without containers, local dev setups drift. One engineer runs Postgres 14, another runs 16. One has Redis, another doesn’t. Docker Compose solves this: everyone runs the same stack, reproducibly, with one command.

## A Typical Stack

```yaml
services:
  backend:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '8000:8000'
    env_file: .env.local
    volumes:
      - .:/app
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: devuser
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: devpass
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
    healthcheck:
      test: ['CMD-SHELL', 'pg_isready -U devuser -d myapp']
      interval: 5s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - '6379:6379'

volumes:
  postgres_data:
```

## Key Patterns

**Use health checks on your database.** Without them, your app container starts before Postgres is ready to accept connections, and you get a cryptic connection refused error. The `condition: service_healthy` in `depends_on` fixes this.

**Bind-mount your source code.** The `volumes: - .:/app` line means file changes on your host are immediately visible inside the container. Combined with a hot-reload server, you get instant feedback without rebuilding.

**Use override files for local extras.** Keep `docker-compose.yml` clean and add dev-only config (like exposing the database port to the host) in `docker-compose.override.yml`, which Compose merges automatically.

## Useful Commands

```bash
# Rebuild and start in the background
docker compose up --build -d

# Shell into the running backend container
docker compose exec backend bash

# Tail logs from all services
docker compose logs -f

# Tear everything down including volumes (wipes the database)
docker compose down -v
```

## Tips for CI

In CI pipelines, add `--wait` to `docker compose up` so your pipeline waits until all health checks pass before running tests. No more flaky test failures caused by Postgres not being ready.
fields.excerpt: Stop the 'works on my machine' problem for good. Here's how to build a reliable local dev environment with Docker Compose, health checks, and override files.

fields.title: PostgreSQL Indexing Strategies That Actually Work
fields.body:
## Start With Diagnostics

Before adding a single index, find your slow queries. Enable `pg_stat_statements` in `postgresql.conf` and then:

```sql
SELECT query, mean_exec_time, calls, total_exec_time
FROM pg_stat_statements
ORDER BY total_exec_time DESC
LIMIT 10;
```

This shows you where time is actually being spent. Fix the top offenders first.

## The EXPLAIN ANALYZE Loop

For each suspect query, run:

```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT * FROM posts
WHERE author_id = 42 AND status = 'published'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 20;
```

A `Seq Scan` on a large table is the main warning sign. Rows removed by filter is the number that shows you how inefficient the scan is.

## Composite Indexes: Column Order Matters

For a filter + sort like the query above:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_author_status_created
ON posts (author_id, status, created_at DESC);
```

Rule: equality columns first, then range or sort columns. Putting `created_at` first would make the index useless for the `author_id = 42` filter.

## Partial Indexes

If 95% of your queries only care about published posts:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_published_posts_created
ON posts (created_at DESC)
WHERE status = 'published';
```

This index is much smaller and faster to scan than a full index. The query planner will use it for any query with `WHERE status = 'published'`.

## Covering Indexes

Avoid heap fetches entirely with `INCLUDE`:

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_author_covering
ON posts (author_id, created_at DESC)
INCLUDE (title, slug, excerpt);
```

Now a listing query can be satisfied from the index alone—no table lookups.

## What to Avoid

- Over-indexing. Every index slows down inserts and updates and consumes disk space.
- Indexing low-cardinality columns like boolean flags in isolation.
- Forgetting `ANALYZE` after bulk data loads—stale statistics lead to bad query plans.
fields.excerpt: Bad indexes (or missing ones) are behind most slow PostgreSQL queries. Here's a systematic approach: from diagnostics to composite, partial, and covering indexes.

fields.title: Building a CI/CD Pipeline with GitHub Actions
fields.body:
## Why GitHub Actions?

GitHub Actions is now the default CI for most open-source and many private projects. It’s tightly integrated with GitHub, has a huge marketplace of reusable actions, and the free tier covers most small teams comfortably.

## A Solid Django Pipeline

```yaml
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_DB: test_db
          POSTGRES_USER: test_user
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_pass
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'
          cache: pip

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install -r requirements.txt

      - name: Run tests
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgres://test_user:test_pass@localhost/test_db
        run: pytest --tb=short -q
```

## Security: Pin Actions to a Commit SHA

Do not use `@v4` tags in production pipelines. Tags are mutable and can be changed by the action author (or an attacker who compromises their account). Pin to a specific commit:

```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683  # v4.2.2
```

Use Dependabot to keep these SHAs updated automatically.

## Caching Dependencies

Cache `pip` or `npm` installs to cut job time significantly:

```yaml
- uses: actions/setup-python@v5
  with:
    python-version: '3.12'
    cache: pip
```

For a medium-sized Python project this alone cuts install time from ~60s to ~5s.

## Deploy Step

Gate deploys on a successful test run and target the main branch only:

```yaml
  deploy:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to Render
        run: curl -X POST ${{ secrets.RENDER_DEPLOY_HOOK }}
```

Store secrets in GitHub repository or organisation secrets—never hardcode them in the workflow file.
fields.excerpt: A step-by-step guide to building a GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline for a Django application, with Postgres services, dependency caching, and secure deployments.

fields.title: TypeScript Generics: A Practical Deep Dive
fields.excerpt: Generics are the feature that separates beginner TypeScript from expert TypeScript. Here's a practical guide from basic type variables through conditional types and infer.

fields.title: Redis Caching Patterns in Django Applications
fields.body:
## Why Cache?

A well-placed cache can reduce database load by 90% on read-heavy endpoints. The key word is well-placed—cache incorrectly and you ship stale data to users.

## Setting Up django-redis

```python
# settings.py
CACHES = {
    'default': {
        'BACKEND': 'django_redis.cache.RedisCache',
        'LOCATION': 'redis://localhost:6379/1',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'CLIENT_CLASS': 'django_redis.client.DefaultClient',
            'KEY_PREFIX': 'myapp_v1',  # bump on breaking schema changes
        },
        'TIMEOUT': 300,  # 5 minutes default
    }
}
```

The `KEY_PREFIX` is important. Bump it when your cached data schema changes and you need an instant, zero-downtime cache invalidation.

## Pattern 1: Cache-Aside

The most common pattern. Check the cache first; on miss, populate from the database:

```python
from django.core.cache import cache

def get_post_detail(post_id: int) -> dict:
    key = f'post:{post_id}'
    data = cache.get(key)
    if data is None:
        post = Post.objects.select_related('author').get(pk=post_id)
        data = PostDetailSerializer(post).data
        cache.set(key, data, timeout=600)
    return data
```

## Pattern 2: Cache Invalidation on Write

When the post is updated, delete the cached version:

```python
from django.db.models.signals import post_save
from django.dispatch import receiver

@receiver(post_save, sender=Post)
def invalidate_post_cache(sender, instance, **kwargs):
    cache.delete(f'post:{instance.pk}')
    cache.delete('post_list_page_1')  # also clear list pages
```

## Pattern 3: Versioned Keys for Rolling Deploys

During a rolling deploy, old and new app instances run simultaneously. If your serializer output changes, old instances may write stale keys that new instances then read:

```python
APP_VERSION = os.environ.get('APP_VERSION', 'dev')

def cache_key(name: str) -> str:
    return f'{APP_VERSION}:{name}'
```

## What Not to Cache

- User-specific data in a shared key (data leaks)
- Data that changes on every request
- Results of non-idempotent operations

Start with the slowest, most-read endpoints and measure before and after. Cache strategically.
fields.excerpt: A practical guide to Redis caching patterns in Django: cache-aside, write-through invalidation, versioned keys for rolling deploys, and what not to cache.

fields.title: Kubernetes for Developers: From Zero to Production Pod
fields.body:
## The Mental Model

Kubernetes is a system for running containers reliably at scale. At its core: you describe the desired state, and Kubernetes continuously works to match reality to that description. The key objects to know are Pods, Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps.

## A Deployment

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
  labels:
    app: backend
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: backend
          image: myapp/backend:1.4.2
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 256Mi
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /api/health/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
```

## Resource Requests and Limits Are Not Optional

Leaving them out is the most common mistake. Without `requests`, the scheduler has no information for placing pods. Without `limits`, a runaway process can starve other pods on the same node. Set both.

## Readiness vs Liveness Probes

**Readiness** controls when a pod receives traffic. If it fails, the pod is removed from the Service endpoint list but kept running.

**Liveness** controls when a pod is restarted. If it fails repeatedly, the kubelet kills and restarts the pod.

Do not reuse the same endpoint for both. Your readiness probe might check upstream dependencies (database, cache), while liveness should only check that the process itself is alive.

## PodDisruptionBudgets

For stateful workloads, always define a PDB to protect against voluntary disruptions during node maintenance:

```yaml
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: backend-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
```

This ensures at least 2 replicas stay running during a node drain.
fields.excerpt: A developer's guide to Kubernetes: the mental model, Deployments, resource limits (don't skip them), readiness vs liveness probes, and PodDisruptionBudgets.

fields.title: Securing Your API: OWASP Top 10 for Developers
fields.body:
## The Threat Landscape

APIs are now the primary attack surface for most web applications. The OWASP API Security Top 10 identifies the most critical risks. Here are the ones I see mishandled most often.

## API1: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA)

The most common and most damaging API vulnerability. You check authentication but forget to check whether the authenticated user is allowed to access *this specific object*:

```python
# VULNERABLE
@api_view(['GET'])
@permission_classes([IsAuthenticated])
def get_document(request, doc_id):
    doc = Document.objects.get(pk=doc_id)  # any user can access any doc
    return Response(DocumentSerializer(doc).data)

# CORRECT
@api_view(['GET'])
@permission_classes([IsAuthenticated])
def get_document(request, doc_id):
    doc = Document.objects.get(pk=doc_id, owner=request.user)
    return Response(DocumentSerializer(doc).data)
```

Always filter by the authenticated user when querying user-owned resources.

## API3: Broken Object Property Level Authorization

Returning more fields than the client should see. Use explicit serializer fields—never `fields = '__all__'` on sensitive models. An API response showing `password_hash`, `internal_notes`, or `stripe_customer_id` to regular users is a common misconfiguration.

## API2: Broken Authentication

- Enforce strong password policies at the API layer, not just the frontend
- Rate-limit login endpoints aggressively (OWASP recommends max 5 attempts per 15 minutes)
- Implement account lockout and alerting on repeated failures
- Never return different error messages for 'user not found' vs 'wrong password'—this enables user enumeration

## API8: Security Misconfiguration

- Disable debug mode in production (`DEBUG=False` in Django)
- Remove default admin credentials and sample routes
- Set strict CORS origins; `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` on an authenticated API is a bug
- Rotate secrets regularly and audit who has access

## Logging Authorization Failures

Log every 401 and 403 response with the user ID, endpoint, and timestamp. Individually they look like noise; aggregated over an hour, an IDOR scan becomes obvious.
fields.excerpt: The OWASP API Security Top 10 explains what attackers target in your API. Here's what BOLA, broken auth, and security misconfiguration look like in Django code, and how to fix them.

fields.title: Microservices vs Monolith: A Practical Decision Framework
fields.body:
## The Default Should Be Monolith

Every microservices architecture I’ve worked on started as an attempt to scale development velocity. Most of them slowed it down instead. Start with a well-structured monolith. Split only when you have a concrete reason.

## When Microservices Actually Help

**Independently scalable bottlenecks.** If your image processing service needs 50 CPU cores but your API needs 2, separating them makes sense.

**Independent deployment requirements.** A payment processor that needs PCI-DSS isolation from the rest of your stack.

**Truly separate teams.** Conway's Law is real: your architecture will mirror your organisation. Separate services only make sense when separate teams own them end-to-end.

**Different language requirements.** An ML inference service in Python sitting behind a Go API is a legitimate split.

## The Hidden Costs

- **Network latency and failures.** Every synchronous call across a service boundary can fail in ways an in-process call cannot.
- **Distributed tracing.** You cannot tail a single log file. You need Jaeger, Zipkin, or equivalent to follow a request across services.
- **Data consistency.** Transactions don't span service boundaries. You're writing event-driven sagas instead of `BEGIN; ... COMMIT;`.
- **Local development complexity.** `docker compose` helps, but simulating service interactions locally is never as clean as running one process.

## The Modular Monolith

A well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries is often the best of both worlds. Enforce boundaries at the code level (Django apps, Python packages), share a single database, and deploy as one process. Split into services later if and when you hit a genuine bottleneck.

```
myapp/
  payments/       # strict boundary: never imported by other modules directly
  notifications/  # communicates via internal events
  core/           # shared models and utilities
```

## The Migration Path

If you do split, the Strangler Fig pattern works well: build the new service alongside the monolith, route a fraction of traffic to it, migrate gradually, then retire the monolith code. Never do a big-bang rewrite.
fields.excerpt: Microservices are not a default. They are a solution to specific scaling and organisational problems that most teams don't have yet. Here's a framework for making the call.

fields.title: React Performance: Beyond useMemo and useCallback
fields.excerpt: Profile before you optimise. Then: virtualise long lists, split contexts by update frequency, and use code splitting. useMemo and useCallback are for later.

fields.title: Python Async/Await Under the Hood
fields.body:
## The Event Loop

Python’s asyncio is single-threaded. There is one event loop running on one OS thread. When your coroutine hits an `await`, it suspends and yields control back to the event loop, which can then run another coroutine. This is cooperative multitasking, not parallel execution.

Implication: CPU-bound work in async code blocks the entire event loop. Use `asyncio.run_in_executor` to offload it to a thread pool.

## Coroutines vs Tasks

A coroutine is just a function defined with `async def`. It does nothing until you await it or schedule it as a Task:

```python
import asyncio

async def fetch(url: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(1)  # simulates I/O
    return f'response from {url}'

# Sequential: ~3 seconds
async def sequential():
    a = await fetch('url1')
    b = await fetch('url2')
    c = await fetch('url3')

# Concurrent: ~1 second
async def concurrent():
    a, b, c = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch('url1'),
        fetch('url2'),
        fetch('url3'),
    )
```

You have to explicitly make things concurrent with `gather`, `TaskGroup`, or `create_task`.

## TaskGroup (Python 3.11+)

`asyncio.TaskGroup` is the modern way to run concurrent tasks with better error handling than `gather`:

```python
async def fetch_all(urls: list[str]) -> list[str]:
    results = []
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tasks = [tg.create_task(fetch(url)) for url in urls]
    return [t.result() for t in tasks]
```

If any task raises, the group cancels all remaining tasks—unlike `gather`, which leaves orphaned tasks running.

## The Blocking Code Trap

```python
# This blocks the event loop for the entire duration of the sleep
async def bad():
    import time
    time.sleep(5)  # blocks everything

# Correct: yield control to the event loop
async def good():
    await asyncio.sleep(5)  # non-blocking
```

Common blocking culprits: `requests` (use `httpx`), `psycopg2` (use `asyncpg`), standard `open()` for large files (use `aiofiles`).

## Verdict

Async Python shines for I/O-bound concurrency: HTTP clients, database queries, file I/O. For CPU-bound work, reach for `multiprocessing` or a compiled extension.
fields.excerpt: Understanding Python's async/await from the ground up: the event loop, coroutines vs tasks, asyncio.gather vs TaskGroup, and the blocking code traps to avoid.

fields.title: Terraform: Managing AWS Infrastructure as Code
fields.body:
## Why Infrastructure as Code?

ClickOps—manually configuring AWS resources through the console—is fast to start and impossible to maintain. After six months you have no idea what you provisioned, why, or how to reproduce it. Terraform gives you version-controlled, reviewable, repeatable infrastructure.

## Project Structure

```
infra/
  environments/
    staging/
      main.tf
      terraform.tfvars
    production/
      main.tf
      terraform.tfvars
  modules/
    ecs-service/
    rds-postgres/
    vpc/
```

Separate state files per environment prevent a staging change from accidentally affecting production.

## A Basic ECS Service

```hcl
module 'api_service' {
  source = '../modules/ecs-service'

  name          = 'api'
  image         = 'myapp/api:${var.image_tag}'
  cpu           = 256
  memory        = 512
  desired_count = 2

  environment_variables = {
    DATABASE_URL = data.aws_ssm_parameter.db_url.value
    REDIS_URL    = data.aws_ssm_parameter.redis_url.value
  }
}
```

## Remote State

Always store state remotely. Local state files are a disaster waiting to happen:

```hcl
terraform {
  backend 's3' {
    bucket         = 'myapp-terraform-state'
    key            = 'production/terraform.tfstate'
    region         = 'us-east-1'
    dynamodb_table = 'terraform-locks'
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```

The DynamoDB table provides state locking so two engineers can’t run `terraform apply` simultaneously.

## The Plan/Apply Workflow

```bash
# Always review the plan before applying
terraform plan -out=tfplan

# Apply the reviewed plan
terraform apply tfplan
```

In CI, post the plan output as a PR comment so your team can review infrastructure changes the same way they review code.

## What to Avoid

- Hardcoding secrets in `.tf` files. Use AWS SSM Parameter Store or Secrets Manager.
- `terraform apply` directly on production without a plan review.
- Sharing a single state file across environments.
fields.excerpt: Stop clicking around the AWS console. Here's how to structure Terraform projects, manage remote state, and build a plan/apply workflow your team can trust.

fields.title: LLM Integration Patterns for Production Applications
fields.body:
## The Gap Between Demo and Production

Every LLM demo looks impressive. Production LLM features are a different story: latency is unpredictable, outputs are non-deterministic, and models hallucinate. Here’s what I’ve learned shipping LLM features in anger.

## Structured Output Is Non-Negotiable

Free-form text output from an LLM is nearly impossible to reliably parse downstream. Use the model’s structured output / JSON mode:

```python
from openai import OpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel

client = OpenAI()

class PostSummary(BaseModel):
    title: str
    key_points: list[str]
    estimated_read_time_minutes: int

def summarise_post(content: str) -> PostSummary:
    response = client.beta.chat.completions.parse(
        model='gpt-4o-mini',
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'Summarise this post: {content}'}],
        response_format=PostSummary,
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.parsed
```

With structured output, reliability goes from ~85% to 99%+.

## Prompt Injection Defence

If any user-provided text reaches your prompt, you are vulnerable to prompt injection. Run a classifier on user input before passing it to the model:

```python
def is_prompt_injection(text: str) -> bool:
    # Simple heuristic; replace with a trained classifier in production
    injection_patterns = [
        'ignore previous instructions',
        'you are now',
        'disregard your',
    ]
    lower = text.lower()
    return any(pattern in lower for pattern in injection_patterns)
```

This adds ~50ms latency but is worth it.

## Latency Management

LLM calls are slow (500ms–10s). Never block a user-facing request on an LLM call unless it’s the core feature. Use background tasks for non-blocking work:

```python
@app.post('/posts/')
async def create_post(post: PostCreate, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks):
    db_post = await create_post_in_db(post)
    background_tasks.add_task(generate_seo_metadata, db_post.id)
    return db_post
```

## Observability

Log every LLM call: input tokens, output tokens, latency, model version, and the full prompt. Token costs compound quickly at scale, and you can’t optimise what you can’t see.

## Caching

For deterministic inputs (e.g. generating a summary of a specific post), cache the LLM response. Most post content doesn’t change, so you pay the LLM cost once and cache for hours.
fields.excerpt: LLM demos are easy. Production LLM features are hard. Structured output, prompt injection defence, latency management, and observability are the four things that matter.

fields.title: System Design: Building a Scalable URL Shortener
fields.body:
## Requirements

**Functional:** Given a long URL, return a short code. Given a short code, redirect to the original URL. Custom aliases optional.

**Non-functional:** 100M URLs created/month. 10B redirects/month (read-heavy, ~100:1 read:write ratio). 99.99% redirect availability. <50ms p99 redirect latency.

## Short Code Generation

Use base62 encoding (a-z, A-Z, 0-9) on a distributed counter or random ID. Six characters gives 62^6 ≈ 56 billion unique codes—more than enough.

```python
import string
import secrets

ALPHABET = string.ascii_letters + string.digits  # base62

def generate_code(length: int = 6) -> str:
    return ''.join(secrets.choice(ALPHABET) for _ in range(length))

def encode_base62(num: int) -> str:
    if num == 0:
        return ALPHABET[0]
    result = []
    while num:
        result.append(ALPHABET[num % 62])
        num //= 62
    return ''.join(reversed(result))
```

## Data Model

```sql
CREATE TABLE short_urls (
    code        VARCHAR(10) PRIMARY KEY,
    long_url    TEXT        NOT NULL,
    owner_id    INTEGER     REFERENCES users(id),
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    expires_at  TIMESTAMPTZ,
    click_count BIGINT      DEFAULT 0
);

CREATE INDEX idx_short_urls_owner ON short_urls (owner_id, created_at DESC);
```

## Caching Strategy

At 10B redirects/month, every redirect hitting Postgres would need ~3,900 QPS sustained. Cache hot codes in Redis:

```python
async def resolve(code: str) -> str | None:
    # L1: Redis cache (sub-millisecond)
    cached = await redis.get(f'url:{code}')
    if cached:
        return cached.decode()

    # L2: Database
    row = await db.fetchrow('SELECT long_url FROM short_urls WHERE code = $1', code)
    if row:
        await redis.setex(f'url:{code}', 3600, row['long_url'])
        return row['long_url']

    return None
```

With a 95% cache hit rate (very achievable for a power-law URL distribution), you need only 195 QPS from Postgres.

## Collision Handling

For random code generation, check for collisions before inserting. A Bloom filter pre-screens used codes at memory cost of ~1.2 bytes/code—far cheaper than a DB roundtrip:

```python
from bloomfilter import BloomFilter

bloom = BloomFilter(capacity=100_000_000, error_rate=0.001)

def find_available_code() -> str:
    while True:
        code = generate_code()
        if code not in bloom:  # probably available
            return code
        # If bloom says it's there, check DB to confirm (rare)
```

## Scaling Reads

For 10B redirects/month at p99 <50ms: a single Redis node handles ~100K QPS. Add read replicas if needed. Behind that, Postgres with connection pooling (PgBouncer) handles the cache misses easily.
fields.excerpt: A step-by-step system design for a URL shortener that handles 10 billion redirects per month: base62 encoding, Redis caching, Bloom filters, and Postgres at scale.

fields.title: Why We Migrated Our Hot Path from Django to Rust
fields.excerpt: We replaced one Django service with Rust: CPU dropped 10x, latency dropped 60x. Here's why we chose Rust over Go, how we designed the boundary, and what we kept in Python.

fields.body: Great intro! One thing worth adding is `response_model` on your endpoints. It filters the output serialisation and prevents accidentally leaking internal fields—super useful once your Pydantic models grow.
Agreed on `response_model`. Pair it with `response_model_exclude_unset=True` and you only serialise fields that were actually set. Huge win for optional fields in PATCH endpoints.
How does FastAPI compare with Litestar (formerly Starlite) for greenfield projects? I've been evaluating both and Litestar's DI container feels more explicit, but FastAPI has the larger ecosystem.
Worth mentioning: FastAPI has solid built-in OAuth2 + JWT support via its security utilities module. It's underrated—you can wire up a full bearer token flow in about 20 lines.

The health check tip is essential. We had so many race conditions between our app container and Postgres until we added `condition: service_healthy`. Wish this had been in every tutorial I read when I started.

I'd add: set resource limits in your Compose file too (`deploy.resources.limits`). Without them your local Postgres can steal memory from your IDE and you spend an hour wondering why your build is slow.

The `pg_stat_statements` tip alone makes this post worth reading. We found a query running 50,000 times per hour with a 200ms average execution time—completely invisible until we enabled this extension.

Also worth knowing: BRIN indexes for time-series or append-only tables. If you have an events table where rows are inserted in timestamp order, a BRIN index is 100x smaller than a B-tree and query time barely changes for date-range scans.
We use BRIN on our audit log table with a 256-page range. Dropped index size from 4GB to 40MB, and date-range queries for compliance reporting are actually faster because the index fits in memory.

Critical security note: pin your actions to a commit SHA, not a tag. Tags are mutable—a compromised action maintainer account can push malicious code to a tag, and your pipeline runs it automatically. Use Dependabot for automated SHA updates.

Absolutely right on the SHA pinning. We also use `permissions: contents: read` at the workflow level and only elevate permissions in specific jobs that need them. Principle of least privilege applies to CI too.

The conditional types section finally clicked for me. I've been fighting with complex TS generics for months and this is the clearest explanation I've seen. The `infer` keyword in particular—never understood what it was for until this post.
The `infer` keyword is one of the most underused features in TypeScript. Would love a follow-up post specifically on template literal types—they're equally powerful and equally poorly documented.

Are you using `django-redis` or going direct with `redis-py`? We've had cache key collision issues across deployments. The `KEY_PREFIX` trick you mentioned is exactly what we needed—we were versioning by hand in every cache call.

We use `django-redis`. The `KEY_PREFIX` in settings is the right place for it—you get it for free on every cache call. We also namespace by app version in the prefix (`myapp_v2`) so rolling deploys don’t serve stale serialised objects.

The section on resource requests and limits needs to be in every Kubernetes intro. Leaving them unset is the most common reason for noisy-neighbour incidents on shared clusters. New engineers especially don't know this is required.

Agree on resource limits. I'd add: don’t skip PodDisruptionBudgets on any workload with more than one replica. We had a node drain during a peak period take out all our replicas simultaneously because we hadn’t set a PDB.

The BOLA section is spot on—object-level auth checks are the most commonly missed vulnerability in every code review I do. The `filter(owner=request.user)` pattern should be in every junior developer's muscle memory.

I'd add to the logging section: aggregate your 403s by user + endpoint over a sliding window. Individually they look like noise. Clustered, a BOLA scan is obvious within minutes. We use this as an alert trigger before the security team even looks at logs.

We did the reverse journey—started with microservices for a greenfield project and spent 8 months consolidating back to a modular monolith. Distributed tracing overhead, local dev complexity, and inter-service latency were all worse than just running one process.

The 'distributed monolith' is the worst outcome: all the operational complexity of microservices with none of the benefits, because the services are still tightly coupled at the data layer. Strong domain boundaries have to come before the split.

The 'profile before optimising' advice is the most important thing in this post and the most ignored. I've seen codebases with `useMemo` on every component that still felt sluggish because the actual bottleneck was a 50ms synchronous localStorage read on mount.

The `asyncio.TaskGroup` vs `gather` distinction on error handling semantics is something I hadn't fully appreciated. `gather` leaving orphaned tasks running on partial failure has bitten us in production—switching to TaskGroup fixed a whole class of subtle bugs.

We moved to Terragrunt for DRY configs across environments. The boilerplate in pure Terraform grows fast once you have more than two environments. Terragrunt's `generate` blocks handle the remote state backend config automatically.

Terraform Stacks (currently in preview) looks like HashiCorp's native answer to the Terragrunt use case. Worth keeping an eye on if you're evaluating long-term. That said, Terragrunt is battle-tested and I wouldn't migrate away from it just yet.

The structured output point is key. We went from ~82% reliable JSON parsing with prompt engineering to 99.6% with the `response_format` parameter. The remaining 0.4% are model refusals, not parse failures—completely different failure mode to handle.

How are you handling prompt injection at scale? The heuristic pattern matching you mentioned works for obvious cases but sophisticated injections slip through. We're evaluating a fine-tuned classifier but the false positive rate on legitimate user queries is still too high.

We run a two-stage check: the heuristic patterns as a fast pre-filter, then a small dedicated classifier model for anything that passes. The classifier adds ~80ms but FPR is under 0.3%. We also sandboxed user input—it never touches the system prompt directly.

The base62 encoding section is clean. We use a similar approach but with a Bloom filter pre-check before the DB insertion. At 100M URLs the collision rate with 6-char codes starts becoming non-trivial—the Bloom filter keeps the retry rate under 0.01%.

Bloom filters are the right call there. What false positive rate are you targeting for the filter? We use 0.1% with ~1.2 bytes per URL—120MB for 100M URLs, well within what fits in Redis memory on a standard instance.
